items.passage: Anglerfish are deep-sea predators famous for the glowing lure that dangles above their head. The light comes from bacteria that live inside the lure and feed on the host. Because sunlight cannot reach these depths, prey is drawn toward the glow and easily captured. Other species use bioluminescence to signal mates or to startle predators that come close. Scientists continue to study these strange partnerships for clues about life in extreme places.

Wildlife corridors are protected strips of land that allow animals to move safely between separate habitats. Without these passages, animal populations become isolated and slowly lose the genetic diversity that long-term survival requires. Engineers build forested bridges over busy highways or planted strips along major rivers and freight railways. Such routes help large predators like bears and wolves reach mates well beyond their immediate range. Conservation groups now treat connectivity as essential to keeping fragmented ecosystems healthy and resilient.

Social loafing describes the tendency for individuals to put in less effort when working as part of a group. This happens when each person feels their personal contribution will not be noticed or measured separately. Research shows that members of large teams often slow down once shared rewards replace individual feedback. Managers can reduce the effect by making each role visible and assigning clear personal targets. These practices restore a sense of accountability and help teams meet ambitious shared goals.

The first transatlantic telegraph cable was laid between Ireland and Newfoundland in the summer of 1858. Earlier attempts had snapped under the strain of deep ocean currents and rough weather. Once messages began flowing, they crossed in minutes rather than the weeks ships had needed. Investors quickly funded longer cables that connected Europe with India and the Americas. These early networks built the foundation for the global communications systems that came after.

Geysers are hot springs that periodically erupt with a column of boiling water and steam. Below ground, water collects in narrow chambers where heat from nearby rock raises the temperature past boiling. Pressure builds because the chamber traps the rising steam against thin layers of harder rock above. When the pressure exceeds what the channel can hold, the trapped water rushes upward and erupts at the surface. After each eruption, the chamber slowly refills and the buildup begins again.

A black hole forms when a massive star collapses and squeezes its remaining matter into an incredibly small space. Inside this region, gravity grows so strong that not even light can escape. Around the edge sits a sharp boundary called the event horizon, the line beyond which nothing returns. Dust and gas pulled inward heat up and form a bright disk that glows for years. Astronomers map these disks to find black holes that otherwise stay invisible.
